skill.name: gitlab
skill.description: Use the glab CLI to inspect and manage GitLab merge requests, issues, CI pipelines, and repositories.
skill.summary: Guide GitLab work through safe, explicit glab CLI commands.
skill.author: ETeissonniere

security_audit.summary: The static Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown command examples and words like description or Desc. The skill is not malicious, but it intentionally guides external glab commands that can modify GitLab resources, trigger CI, and clone repositories, so publication should include an operational risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Mutating GitLab CLI Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents glab commands that can create, merge, approve, close, or comment on GitLab resources. These commands are legitimate for a GitLab helper, but they require explicit user confirmation before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly list glab commands that change merge requests and issues. The behavior is intentional and not malicious, but it can affect remote project state.

medium_findings.title: CI Pipeline Execution Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes commands to run and retry GitLab CI pipelines. Triggering CI can consume runner resources or deploy code depending on the repository configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command reference and behavioral guideline explicitly mention status checks, running CI, and retrying failed CI. The risk depends on repository permissions and pipeline configuration.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution False Positive
low_findings.description: The analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the evidence is Markdown inline code and fenced bash examples. No executable Ruby code or dynamic shell wrapper is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only file is Markdown documentation, and the backticks delimit command examples rather than executable code. No evidence found of Ruby execution syntax in an executable file.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections appear to match ordinary description text and placeholder words such as Desc. No cryptographic algorithm use is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain metadata and glab command examples, not calls to cryptographic libraries or algorithms. No evidence found of encryption, hashing, or signature logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: External glab Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs an assistant to use glab commands for GitLab operations. Commands should be reviewed with the user before any operation that changes remote state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced command tables explicitly list external CLI commands. The pattern is expected for this skill, but it is security-relevant because several commands mutate remote state.

content.value_statement: GitLab work often requires switching between issues, merge requests, pipelines, and repository pages. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear glab commands for common GitLab tasks.
actual_capabilities: Detects GitLab repositories by checking Git remotes for GitLab hosts.
Checks glab authentication status before GitLab operations.
Provides glab commands for creating, listing, viewing, approving, checking out, and merging merge requests.
Provides glab commands for creating, listing, viewing, closing, and commenting on issues.
Provides glab commands for CI status, pipeline lists, traces, runs, and retries.
Provides repository commands for viewing, cloning, and opening projects in a browser.
limitations: Requires the glab CLI to be installed and authenticated.
Does not validate project-specific permissions or branch protection rules.
Does not include safeguards for deployment pipelines beyond checking status first.
Does not provide GitLab API examples or custom scripting patterns.

use_cases.title: Review merge request status
use_cases.description: Inspect open merge requests, view comments, and check out a merge request branch for local review.

use_cases.title: Triage GitLab issues
use_cases.description: List assigned issues, view issue details, add comments, and close resolved items from the terminal.

use_cases.title: Check CI pipeline health
use_cases.description: Review pipeline status, inspect traces, and decide whether a failed pipeline should be retried.

prompt_templates.title: Check GitLab setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether this repository is hosted on GitLab and verify my glab authentication status.

prompt_templates.title: Review a merge request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use glab to view merge request <id> with comments, then summarize the open review points.

prompt_templates.title: Triage assigned issues
prompt_templates.prompt: List my assigned GitLab issues and help me choose which issue needs attention first.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate CI failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Check the current GitLab CI status, inspect the failing job trace, and recommend whether to retry the pipeline.

output_examples.input: Check whether this project uses GitLab.
output_examples.output: The assistant verifies Git remotes, identifies a GitLab host when present, and checks glab authentication before suggesting next actions.
The assistant retrieves the merge request, includes discussion context, and summarizes approval state, unresolved threads, and likely next steps.

output_examples.input: Why did the latest pipeline fail?
output_examples.output: The assistant checks CI status, reviews available trace output, and explains the failure with a cautious recommendation about retrying.

best_practices: Confirm the active Git remote and GitLab host before running glab commands.
Ask for explicit user approval before merge, close, run, retry, clone, or approve operations.
Use read-only glab commands first when investigating issues, merge requests, or CI failures.
anti_patterns: Do not merge or approve merge requests without clear user confirmation.
Do not run or retry CI pipelines before checking current pipeline status.
Do not assume every GitLab project has the same permissions, labels, or deployment behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill require glab?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written around the GitLab glab CLI and expects it to be installed.

faq.question: Can it work with self-hosted GitLab?
faq.answer: Yes, if the Git remote points to a known self-hosted GitLab instance and glab is configured for that host.

faq.question: Does it make changes automatically?
faq.answer: The skill lists commands that can make changes, but users should approve mutating operations before execution.

faq.question: Can it inspect merge request comments?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends the comments flag when full merge request context is needed.

faq.question: Can it troubleshoot GitLab CI failures?
faq.answer: It can guide glab commands for status, pipeline lists, and traces, then summarize likely failure causes.

faq.question: Does it replace GitLab permissions?
faq.answer: No. All operations still depend on the authenticated user's GitLab permissions and project rules.
